metadata.title: Neurologic Safety of Recombinant Human Bone Morphogenetic Protein-2 in Posterolateral Lumbar Fusion Complicated by Dural Tear
metadata.journal: The spine journal : official journal of the North American Spine Society
metadata.abstract: BACKGROUND CONTEXT: Potential complications related to ectopic bone formation, as seen with transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion, have always been a concern with the use of bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs). Although less clearly anticipated, complications related to the proinflammatory effects of recombinant human bone morphogenetic protein-2 (rhBMP-2), such as swelling and edema in the cervical spine, have been observed as well. Until recently, risks related to intradural exposure to BMP have not been widely considered. However, a recent animal study reports "in the presence of a SCI and/or dural tear, rhBMP-2 diffuses intrathecally and activates a signaling cascade in all major CNS cell types, which may increase glial scarring and impact neurologic recovery." Although this study was conducted at the spinal cord level, the observation generates obvious concerns for the much more common scenario of a dural tear associated with lumbar decompression and fusion. PURPOSE: The purpose of this study was to look for any indication of neurologic injury or impaired neurologic recovery in patients treated with rhBMP-2 for lumbar fusion complicated by dural tear. STUDY DESIGN/SETTING: Propensity score matched case-control study. PATIENT SAMPLE: From consecutive series of 1,037 patients who underwent decompression and posterolateral lumbar spine fusion using rhBMP-2/absorbable collagen sponge between 2003 and 2006, intraoperative dural tear was reported in 58 cases (5.59%). OUTCOME MEASURES: Preoperative and 2-year postoperative Oswestry Disability Index, Short Form-36 (SF-36), leg pain, and back pain scores. METHODS: Fifty-eight cases in which decompression and posterolateral spinal fusion were complicated by dural tear, where propensity score matched to a group without dural tear, based on age, smoking status, number of surgical levels and preoperative Oswestry Disability Index, SF-36 Physical Composite Summary score, SF-36 Mental Composite Summary (MCS) score, and back and leg pain scores. The patients with a dural tear were then compared with the matched cohort with regard to baseline and 2-year patient-based outcome measures. Particular attention was given to indices of leg pain that might reflect an influence of rhBMP-2 on neurologic function or impaired neurologic recovery. RESULTS: No patient in the group with a dural tear and three patients in the group without a dural tear complained of new onset radiculopathy postoperatively, with one requiring oral steroids. The radiculopathy resolved within 6 months postoperatively in all three patients. Statistically significant improvement was observed in all health-related quality of life (HRQOL) measures, except SF-36 MCS, at both 1 and 2 years postoperatively in both groups. There were no significant differences in any HRQOL parameter between the groups with or without a dural tear at either 1 or 2 years postoperatively. In particular, the leg pain improvement, 2.2 points in the group with a dural tear and 2.4 points in the group without a dural tear, was statistically equivalent. CONCLUSIONS: The data suggests fairly convincingly that the presence of a repairable dural tear is not necessarily an impediment to the use of rhBMP-2 in posterolateral fusion. Further studies are needed to address the less common clinical scenario of BMP use in conjunction with spinal cord injury, as studied in the animal model that prompted this investigation. Finally, avoidance of BMP use may still be prudent in the setting of an unrepairable dural tear.

spine_metadata.main_conclusion: Repairable dural tear during posterolateral lumbar fusion with rhBMP-2 does not result in neurologic injury or impaired recovery. BMP use remains safe in this setting, though caution is advised for unrepairable dural tears.

pico.population: Adults (age-matched cohort) undergoing posterolateral lumbar decompression and fusion with rhBMP-2/ACS between 2003-2006; 58 patients with intraoperative dural tear matched to 58 without dural tear
pico.intervention: Posterolateral lumbar fusion with rhBMP-2/absorbable collagen sponge (ACS) in patients with dural tear
pico.comparison: Posterolateral lumbar fusion with rhBMP-2/ACS without dural tear
pico.outcome: ODI, SF-36 (Physical and Mental Composite Scores), leg pain, back pain, new onset radiculopathy

patient_cohorts.name: Dural Tear Group
patient_cohorts.diagnosis: Lumbar stenosis and/or disc herniation with intraoperative dural tear

patient_cohorts.name: No Dural Tear Group (Propensity-Matched Control)
patient_cohorts.diagnosis: Lumbar stenosis and/or disc herniation without intraoperative dural tear

effect_measure.label: 0% in dural tear group vs 5.2% in control

outcomes.name: Leg Pain (Visual Analog Scale or numeric score)

effect_measure.label: Leg pain improvement equivalent between groups (2.2 vs 2.4 points)
Both groups showed statistically significant ODI improvement at 1 and 2 years, with no differences between groups
Both groups showed statistically significant SF-36 PCS improvement, no between-group differences
SF-36 MCS showed no significant improvement in either group
Both groups showed significant back pain improvement with no between-group differences

important_citations.citation_text: in the presence of a SCI and/or dural tear, rhBMP-2 diffuses intrathecally and activates a signaling cascade in all major CNS cell types, which may increase glial scarring and impact neurologic recovery
important_citations.importance_reason: This animal study raised concerns about BMP safety with dural tear, which prompted the current investigation. The current study contradicts this concern for repairable dural tears in lumbar fusion.

chunks.content: BACKGROUND: Bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) have been associated with complications including ectopic bone formation and proinflammatory effects such as swelling and edema. A recent animal study demonstrated that rhBMP-2 diffuses intrathecally in the presence of spinal cord injury and/or dural tear, activating signaling cascades that may increase glial scarring and impair neurologic recovery. These concerns prompted investigation into whether similar neurologic complications occur in lumbar decompression and fusion with rhBMP-2 when complicated by dural tear.
chunks.summary: BMP-related complications and animal study concerns about intradural BMP exposure with dural tears prompted investigation into clinical neurologic safety.

chunks.content: From a consecutive series of 1,037 patients who underwent decompression and posterolateral lumbar spine fusion using rhBMP-2/absorbable collagen sponge (ACS) between 2003 and 2006, intraoperative dural tear was reported in 58 cases (5.59%). These 58 patients were propensity score matched to 58 patients without dural tear based on age, smoking status, number of surgical levels, preoperative Oswestry Disability Index, SF-36 Physical Composite Summary score, SF-36 Mental Composite Summary score, and baseline back and leg pain scores.
chunks.summary: Study enrolled 58 patients with dural tear and 58 propensity-matched controls without dural tear from 1,037 consecutive posterolateral lumbar fusion cases.

chunks.content: Outcome measures included preoperative and 2-year postoperative assessments of Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Short Form-36 (SF-36) Physical Composite Summary score, SF-36 Mental Composite Summary (MCS) score, leg pain, and back pain. Particular attention was given to indices of leg pain to assess for any influence of rhBMP-2 on neurologic function or impaired neurologic recovery in patients with dural tear exposure.
chunks.summary: Primary outcome measures were functional scores (ODI, SF-36) and pain measures, with emphasis on leg pain as indicator of neurologic status.

chunks.content: PURPOSE: The purpose of this study was to look for any indication of neurologic injury or impaired neurologic recovery in patients treated with rhBMP-2 for lumbar fusion complicated by dural tear.
chunks.summary: Primary study objective was to determine if repairable dural tears with rhBMP-2 exposure result in neurologic injury or impaired recovery.

chunks.content: No patient in the group with a dural tear reported new onset radiculopathy postoperatively, compared to three patients (5.2%) in the group without a dural tear. One of the three patients in the control group required oral steroids for radiculopathy management. All three cases of new onset radiculopathy resolved within 6 months postoperatively.
chunks.summary: Zero new onset radiculopathy in dural tear group vs 5.2% (3/58) in control group; all resolved within 6 months.

effect_measure.label: New onset radiculopathy: 0% (0/58) dural tear group vs 5.2% (3/58) control group

chunks.content: Statistically significant improvement was observed in all health-related quality of life (HRQOL) measures except SF-36 Mental Composite Score (MCS), at both 1 and 2 years postoperatively in both groups. This included improvements in Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), SF-36 Physical Composite Summary (PCS) score, leg pain, and back pain. There were no significant differences in any HRQOL parameter between the groups with or without a dural tear at either 1 or 2 years postoperatively.
chunks.summary: Both groups showed significant functional and pain improvements at 1-2 years with no between-group differences; SF-36 MCS showed no significant change.

effect_measure.label: Significant HRQOL improvement in both groups at 1 and 2 years (ODI, SF-36 PCS, pain) with no between-group differences

chunks.content: Leg pain improvement was statistically equivalent between groups: 2.2 points in the dural tear group and 2.4 points in the group without dural tear. This equivalent improvement in leg pain, which is the most sensitive indicator of neurologic function, provides evidence that rhBMP-2 exposure through a dural tear does not impair neurologic recovery.
chunks.summary: Equivalent leg pain improvement (2.2 vs 2.4 points) between dural tear and control groups, indicating preserved neurologic recovery.

effect_measure.label: Leg pain improvement equivalent: 2.2 points (dural tear) vs 2.4 points (control), statistically equivalent

chunks.content: CONCLUSIONS: The data suggests fairly convincingly that the presence of a repairable dural tear is not necessarily an impediment to the use of rhBMP-2 in posterolateral fusion. The study found no evidence of neurologic injury or impaired neurologic recovery in patients with dural tear exposure to rhBMP-2. Further studies are needed to address the less common clinical scenario of BMP use in conjunction with spinal cord injury, as studied in the animal model that prompted this investigation. Finally, avoidance of BMP use may still be prudent in the setting of an unrepairable dural tear.
chunks.summary: Repairable dural tears do not contraindicate rhBMP-2 use in posterolateral fusion, with no evidence of neurologic injury; caution recommended for unrepairable tears.

chunks.content: This study was motivated by a recent animal study demonstrating that rhBMP-2 diffuses intrathecally in the presence of spinal cord injury (SCI) and/or dural tear, activating signaling cascades in all major central nervous system (CNS) cell types. These cascades may increase glial scarring and impact neurologic recovery. Although the animal model examined spinal cord-level exposure, the findings generated clinical concerns for the much more common scenario of dural tear associated with lumbar decompression and fusion.
chunks.summary: Animal study evidence of BMP intradural diffusion and CNS activation motivated investigation into clinical safety of BMP with lumbar dural tears.

chunks.content: Between 2003 and 2006, 1,037 consecutive patients underwent decompression and posterolateral lumbar spine fusion using rhBMP-2/absorbable collagen sponge. This time period captured a cohort of patients in a high-volume spine surgery center. The study population represented a consecutive series without selection bias regarding BMP use or dural tear occurrence.
chunks.summary: Consecutive cohort of 1,037 patients undergoing posterolateral lumbar fusion with rhBMP-2 from 2003-2006 at a high-volume center.

chunks.content: Propensity score matching was performed using multiple baseline variables to create comparable groups: age, smoking status, number of surgical levels, preoperative Oswestry Disability Index, SF-36 Physical Composite Summary score, SF-36 Mental Composite Summary score, and baseline back and leg pain scores. This rigorous matching strategy minimized potential confounding variables and selection bias, allowing direct comparison of outcomes related to dural tear and rhBMP-2 exposure.
chunks.summary: Propensity score matching on 8 baseline variables created balanced comparison groups for assessing dural tear-specific effects.

chunks.content: The study design was a propensity score matched case-control study, which represents an observational study type. While case-control studies provide lower-level evidence compared to randomized trials, the propensity score matching approach strengthens the design by reducing selection bias and creating comparable baseline groups. This design allowed assessment of neurologic outcomes in a clinically relevant scenario (dural tear during lumbar fusion) that would be unethical to randomize.
chunks.summary: Propensity score matched case-control design reduces bias in observational comparison of dural tear outcomes with rhBMP-2.

chunks.content: Back pain improvement was observed in both the dural tear group and the control group at 1 and 2-year follow-up, with statistically significant reductions in pain scores. There were no significant differences in the magnitude of back pain improvement between groups. Combined with equivalent leg pain improvement across groups, the data indicate that pain relief was comparable regardless of dural tear status or rhBMP-2 intradural exposure.
chunks.summary: Equivalent back pain improvement in both groups at follow-up, supporting safety of rhBMP-2 use with dural tears.

effect_measure.label: Equivalent back pain improvement between groups at 1 and 2 years

chunks.content: The Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) is a 10-item self-report questionnaire assessing functional disability in patients with low back pain, with scores ranging from 0-100 where higher scores indicate greater disability. Both the dural tear group and control group demonstrated statistically significant improvements in ODI at 1-year and 2-year follow-ups. The lack of significant between-group difference in ODI improvement indicates that functional recovery was not impaired by dural tear or rhBMP-2 intradural exposure.
chunks.summary: ODI improved significantly in both groups without between-group differences, indicating preserved functional recovery with dural tear.

chunks.content: The SF-36 Physical Composite Summary (PCS) score improved significantly in both the dural tear and control groups at 1 and 2-year postoperative assessments. The SF-36 Mental Composite Summary (MCS) score showed no significant improvement in either group. The parallel improvement patterns and absence of between-group differences in SF-36 PCS indicate equivalent physical health-related quality of life improvements despite dural tear exposure to rhBMP-2.
chunks.summary: SF-36 PCS improved significantly in both groups with no between-group differences; MCS unchanged in both groups.

effect_measure.label: SF-36 PCS: significant improvement in both groups at 1 and 2 years, no between-group differences; SF-36 MCS: no significant change

chunks.content: Among the three patients in the control group who developed new onset radiculopathy postoperatively, one patient required oral corticosteroid therapy for symptom management. All three cases of radiculopathy resolved completely within 6 months of surgery. This resolution pattern is typical for postoperative radiculopathy unrelated to intradural BMP exposure, suggesting the cases were likely related to other postoperative factors such as inflammation or temporary nerve irritation.
chunks.summary: Three control patients developed radiculopathy, one requiring steroids, all resolved within 6 months postoperatively.

chunks.content: The key finding of zero new onset radiculopathy in the dural tear group compared to 5.2% in the control group is particularly significant given that the dural tear group had intradural exposure to rhBMP-2. If BMP had caused intradural inflammation, glial scarring, or CNS activation as suggested by the animal model, one would expect to observe neurologic complications in the dural tear group at least equivalent to or exceeding the control group. The absence of any radiculopathy in the dural tear group suggests rhBMP-2 does not exert clinically significant intradural neurotoxic effects in the setting of lumbar fusion.
chunks.summary: Zero radiculopathy in dural tear group despite BMP exposure contradicts animal study concerns, suggesting clinical safety.

effect_measure.label: Radiculopathy incidence: 0% dural tear group vs 5.2% control group

chunks.content: CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS: The findings of this study have important clinical implications for spine surgeons who use rhBMP-2 in posterolateral lumbar fusion. When an intraoperative dural tear occurs during decompression and fusion surgery, the presence of a repairable tear should not automatically preclude the use of BMP. The study data suggest that repairing the dural tear adequately provides sufficient protection against significant intradural BMP exposure and associated neurologic complications. However, in the uncommon scenario of an unrepairable dural tear, caution with BMP use remains appropriate pending further evidence.
chunks.summary: Repairable dural tears do not contraindicate BMP use; caution recommended only for unrepairable tears.

chunks.content: STUDY LIMITATIONS: The study is limited by its retrospective, non-randomized case-control design, which represents lower-level evidence than prospective randomized controlled trials. Additionally, the study was conducted during 2003-2006 and may not reflect current surgical techniques, perioperative protocols, or patient populations. The matching was based on available baseline variables, and unmeasured confounding cannot be completely excluded. Furthermore, the study examined only repairable dural tears; outcomes in unrepairable dural tears were not assessed. The relatively small sample size (58 matched pairs) limits statistical power for detecting small differences between groups.
chunks.summary: Study limitations include retrospective design, temporal considerations, and focus on repairable dural tears only.

chunks.content: FUTURE DIRECTIONS: The authors recommend further studies to address the less common clinical scenario of BMP use in conjunction with spinal cord injury, as examined in the animal model that prompted this investigation. Additional prospective research comparing outcomes in patients with repaired versus unrepairable dural tears receiving BMP would strengthen the evidence base. Long-term follow-up studies beyond 2 years may reveal delayed neurologic complications or functional differences. Additionally, investigation of different BMP formulations, dosages, and delivery methods in the setting of dural compromise would provide valuable clinical guidance.
chunks.summary: Future research should address BMP safety with spinal cord injury, unrepairable dural tears, and long-term outcomes.
